input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **system under study** in the provided context pertains to **complex engineered systems** (e.g., industrial, cyber-physical, or manufacturing systems) typically developed using **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies. Key characteristics include:

- **Type of System**: Heterogeneous, multi-layered systems with **functional, logical, and physical architectures**, often spanning **requirements, functions, logic, and physics (RFLP)** layers as per [18, 19].
- **Main Components**:
  - **Components**: Described via **component-connector models** (e.g., ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25, 26], or SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs) [17, 27]), capturing **interfaces, interconnections, and composition**.
  - **Behavioral Models**: Supplemented with **state machines** (SysML v1.7), **automata** (MontiArc), or **simulation tools** (e.g., MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation engines [29, 30]).
  - **Physical Layer**: Represents the **product/technical layer** with detailed **physical attributes** and **real-time operational data**.
- **Operational Domain**: Primarily **manufacturing and industrial systems**, where digital shadows (as per Kritzinger et al. [32]) aggregate **contextual data traces** for specific purposes (e.g., monitoring, analytics, or decision support).
- **Key Suitability for Digital Twinning**:
  - **Complexity Handling**: Requires **multi-abstraction modeling** (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM [20], SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]) to manage heterogeneity.
  - **Stakeholder Integration**: Supports **multi-perspective modeling** (e.g., requirements, functional, logical, and physical views) to align diverse stakeholders [31].
  - **Data-Driven**: Relies on **real-time or near-real-time data flows** between physical systems and digital representations, enabling **bidirectional or unidirectional (shadow) data synchronization**.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** in the context provides **active services** to complement or enhance the **physical system** and **users**, differentiated from passive **digital shadows** (which only enable one-way data flow [32]). Key services include:

- **Service Types**:
  - **Monitoring**: Aggregates and abstracts **contextual data traces** (as per Kritzinger et al. [32]) for real-time or historical analysis of the physical system’s state.
  - **Optimization**: Leverages **model-based predictions** (e.g., via SysML, Simulink, or other simulation tools [28, 29, 30]) to suggest improvements in system performance, resource allocation, or operational efficiency.
  - **Prediction**: Uses **behavioral models** (state machines, automata, or physics-based simulations) to forecast system behavior, failures, or performance degradation.
  - **Control**: Acts as an **active intermediary** (unlike digital shadows) to **push data or commands back to the physical system** for autonomous or semi-autonomous decision-making (e.g., via embedded software or IoT gateways).
  - **Visualization**: Provides **multi-abstraction views** (e.g., functional, logical, or physical layers) using **SysML, IBDs, or other MBSE tools** to enable stakeholders to interact with the DT’s models.

- **Target Users**:
  - **Engineers**: For **system design validation**, **requirements verification**, or **architectural trade-off analysis**.
  - **Operational Teams**: For **real-time monitoring**, **troubleshooting**, or **maintenance planning**.
  - **Decision-Makers**: For **strategic optimization** (e.g., production scheduling, resource allocation) based on DT-generated insights.

- **Service Capabilities**:
  - **Bidirectional Data Flow**: Unlike digital shadows, DTs enable **active feedback loops** (e.g., via simulation engines or control algorithms) to influence the physical system.
  - **Model Integration**: Combines **architecture description languages (ADLs)** (e.g., AADL, MontiArc) with **behavioral models** (e.g., Simulink, state machines) for holistic system representation.
  - **Heterogeneous Tool Support**: Integrates **multiple modeling notations** (SysML, ADLs) and **general-purpose languages** (C++, Java) for interoperability.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin implementation** relies on a **heterogeneous toolchain** and **technological enablers** to support **modeling, simulation, data aggregation, and active services**. Key components include:

- **Software Platforms and Frameworks**:
  - **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) Tools**:
    - **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)**: For **functional and logical architecture modeling**, including **Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** [17, 27] to describe components, interfaces, and connectors.
    - **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)**: Such as **ACME [23]**, **AADL [24]**, or **MontiArc [25, 26]**, which provide **component-connector models** with support for **behavioral specifications** (e.g., MontiArc Automaton, state machines in SysML v1.7).
  - **Simulation Engines**:
    - **MathWorks Simulink [28]**: For **detailed behavioral modeling** and **dynamic system simulation**.
    - **Other Simulation Tools/Methods**: General-purpose or domain-specific simulators (e.g., [29, 30]) to validate **physical-layer behaviors** or **control algorithms**.
  - **General-Purpose Languages**:
    - **C++/Java**: For **custom logic implementation**, **embedded control systems**, or **DT service backends**.

- **Data and Storage Technologies**:
  - **Databases**: Not explicitly mentioned in the document, but implied for **storing contextual data traces** (as per digital shadow definitions [32]) and **aggregated DT models**. Likely includes **relational databases** or **NoSQL solutions** for scalability.
  - **Data Aggregation Layers**: Enables **real-time or batch processing** of **sensor data** and **physical system telemetry** to feed into DT models.

- **Specialized Technologies**:
  - **Model Integration Platforms**: To **unify heterogeneous models** (e.g., SysML, ADLs, simulation outputs) into a cohesive DT representation.
  - **IoT/Edge Gateways**: For **bidirectional data exchange** between the physical system and DT (unlike digital shadows, which are unidirectional [32, 33, 34]).
  - **Active Processing Components**: Embedded within the DT to **interpret data**, **execute control logic**, or **generate predictions** (e.g., via simulation or machine learning models, though ML is not explicitly mentioned).

- **Methodologies and Standards**:
  - **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**: A **multi-abstraction modeling framework** [18, 19] to structure DT development across layers.
  - **SMArDT [22]**: A **Digital Twin-specific methodology** for aligning **physical and digital representations** in complex systems.
  - **MBSE Workflows**: Iterative processes to **validate models** against requirements and **refine architectures** using tools like SysML or ADLs.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system described in the provided documents emphasizes **temporal synchronization** through **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, which often involve **multi-level abstraction** (e.g., requirements, functional, logical, and physical layers) to manage complexity. While explicit synchronization frequencies or latency requirements are not detailed, the integration of **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like **SysML IBDs**, **AADL**, or **MontiArc**—combined with **behavioral modeling tools** (e.g., **SysML state machines**, **MathWorks Simulink**, or **general-purpose simulation tools**)—implies **real-time or near-real-time data flows** for active Digital Twin components. The **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (Kritzinger et al., [32]) suggests **one-way data aggregation** with **contextual granularity** (e.g., traces, abstraction layers) but does not specify temporal granularity. For Digital Twins, the system may require **latency-sensitive coordination** (e.g., <100ms for control loops) when integrating with **active components** (e.g., interpreters or processors) to enable bidirectional data flow, though exact time scales are not quantified in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system supports **hierarchical multiplicities** through **multi-layered MBSE abstractions** (e.g., **RFLP**, **OOSEM**, or **SMArDT** methodologies), which decompose systems into **requirements, functions, logic, and physical layers**. **Centralized vs. decentralized architectures** are implied by the use of **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like **ACME**, **AADL**, or **MontiArc**, which model **component-connector systems** with **compositional hierarchies**. For Digital Twins, **multiple instances** may exist (e.g., one per physical asset or per abstraction layer), but explicit **coordination mechanisms** (e.g., **event-driven**, **publish-subscribe**, or **distributed consensus protocols**) are not detailed. Responsibilities are scoped by **abstraction level** (e.g., logical twins handling high-level functions, physical twins managing sensor-actuator interactions), though **decentralized autonomy** (e.g., federated twins) is not explicitly addressed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The **Digital Twin constellation** is orchestrated via **MBSE frameworks** integrating **heterogeneous modeling tools** (e.g., **SysML**, **Simulink**, **C++/Java**) and **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like **MontiArc Automaton** or **SysML state machines** for behavior modeling. **Component integration** relies on **component-connector paradigms** (e.g., **ACME**, **AADL**), where **interfaces**, **interconnections**, and **composition** define system structure. **Resource management** is implied through **layered abstraction** (e.g., logical twins managing computational resources for physical twins), but **system-wide coordination** lacks explicit protocols (e.g., **DDS**, **MQTT**, or **gRPC**). The **Digital Shadow Reference Model** ([32]) suggests **passive aggregation** of data traces, while Digital Twins may include **active components** (e.g., interpreters) for **bidirectional data flow**, though **orchestration patterns** (e.g., **microservices**, **event-driven architectures**) are not specified.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Horizontal integration is facilitated through **interoperability standards** and **heterogeneous data exchange protocols** implied by the **MBSE ecosystem**. The system integrates **architecture description languages (ADLs)** (e.g., **SysML**, **AADL**) with **simulation tools** (e.g., **Simulink**, **general-purpose languages like C++/Java**) and **external enterprise systems** via **model-based interfaces**. **Dependencies on external systems** (e.g., IoT platforms, ERP systems) are not detailed, but the **Digital Shadow Reference Model** ([32]) highlights **one-way data flows** for contextual aggregation, suggesting **APIs** or **message brokers** (e.g., **REST**, **SOAP**) for integration. **Interoperability standards** may include **OPC UA**, **ROS**, or **industry-specific protocols** (e.g., **MODBUS**), though explicit mention is absent. The system does not explicitly describe **federated Digital Twin networks** or **cross-organizational integration patterns**.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin system incorporates **heterogeneous computational models and data components** structured across multiple abstraction layers (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers) to represent system behavior and structure. **Model types** include:
- **Geometric models** (implicitly referenced via architecture description languages like SysML IBDs or AADL for component interfaces/composition),
- **Behavioral models** (described via state machines in SysML v1.7 or MontiArc Automaton, integrated with simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink or general-purpose languages such as C++/Java for detailed dynamics),
- **Analytical models** (embedded within simulation environments for performance/physics-based analysis).

**Data sources** are aggregated from physical sensors, IoT devices, and external databases, while **data management approaches** involve:
- **Aggregation and abstraction** of contextual data traces (as defined in the Digital Shadow Reference Model [32]) for specific purposes (e.g., manufacturing analytics),
- **Tool integration** across SysML, ADLs (e.g., AADL, MontiArc), and simulation platforms to maintain model consistency.

**Model relationships** are explicitly defined via **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like ACME, AADL, or MontiArc, which describe components, connectors, interfaces, and composition hierarchies. Behavioral models are linked to structural models via **state machines** or **automata** (e.g., MontiArc Automaton) to enable synchronized simulation and control. **Data roles** include real-time sensor feeds for validation, historical data for trend analysis, and synthetic data for testing/what-if scenarios.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The Digital Twin system relies on a **heterogeneous sensing infrastructure** characterized by:
- **Sensor types**: Physical sensors (e.g., temperature, pressure, vibration) integrated into manufacturing or operational environments, though specific types are not detailed in the document.
- **Measurement capabilities**: Aligned with system requirements (e.g., functional/physical layers), though exact metrics (e.g., range, units) are not specified.
- **Spatial distribution**: Sensors are deployed to capture contextual data traces for aggregation (as per the Digital Shadow Reference Model [32]), implying distributed placement across the physical system.
- **Sampling frequencies**: Not explicitly mentioned, but implied to be configurable for real-time data collection (e.g., milliseconds to seconds, depending on system dynamics).
- **Data transmission rates**: Assumed to be optimized for low-latency communication (e.g., via IoT protocols or industrial Ethernet) to support active Digital Twin components (e.g., [35]–[37]).
- **Accuracy/precision specifications**: Not quantified, but tied to model validation requirements (e.g., SysML/ADL-defined tolerances for component behavior).
- **Data collection mechanisms**: Automated, one-way data flows from physical objects to digital representations (as per [32]), with no bidirectional control (distinguishing Digital Shadows from Digital Twins). Data is aggregated and abstracted for specific purposes (e.g., manufacturing analytics).
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The Digital Twin system enforces **model fidelity requirements** through multi-layered validation and verification (V&V) methodologies, including:
- **Model fidelity**: Achieved via **abstraction hierarchies** (e.g., RFLP [18], OOSEM [20]) to ensure alignment between requirements, functional/logical architectures, and physical layers. **Architecture description languages (ADLs)** like AADL or MontiArc enforce structural fidelity by defining component interfaces, connectors, and composition rules.
- **Validation methods**: Combination of **formal verification** (e.g., state machine analysis in SysML v1.7) and **simulation-based testing** (e.g., MathWorks Simulink for behavioral dynamics). Uncertainty quantification is implied via **data aggregation and abstraction** (as per [32]) to reconcile real-time sensor data with model predictions.
- **Uncertainty sources**: Inherent in **sensor noise**, model parameter drift, and environmental variability. Quantification relies on **contextual data traces** (e.g., manufacturing analytics) to identify deviations.
- **Error detection/correction**: Active components of the Digital Twin (e.g., [35]–[37]) may employ **closed-loop mechanisms** (e.g., feedback from simulation tools) to correct discrepancies, though explicit protocols are not detailed.
- **Quality assurance processes**: Enforced via **MBSE methodologies** (e.g., SysML, ADLs) to ensure traceability across abstraction layers. **Tool integration** (e.g., Simulink + SysML) supports cross-verification of models and data.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The physical-to-virtual interaction in Digital Twin systems involves a **unidirectional, automated data flow** from physical objects to their digital representations, characterized by the following technical aspects:
  - **Data Types Transmitted**: Contextual data traces (e.g., sensor readings, operational metrics, environmental parameters) aggregated and abstracted for specific purposes, often structured as time-series or event-based datasets.
  - **Communication Protocols**: Standardized protocols such as **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)**, **OPC UA (Open Platform Communications Unified Architecture)**, or **RESTful APIs** for real-time or periodic data transmission.
  - **Transmission Frequencies**: Varies based on system requirements (e.g., high-frequency for real-time monitoring or low-frequency for batch processing), often configured via **event triggers** (e.g., threshold breaches, scheduled intervals) or **polling mechanisms**.
  - **Data Quality Assurance**: Includes **preprocessing steps** like normalization, filtering, and anomaly detection (e.g., using statistical methods or machine learning models) to ensure accuracy and consistency before aggregation.
  - **Data Integration Mechanisms**: Utilizes **model-based systems engineering (MBSE) tools** (e.g., SysML for architecture modeling) and **heterogeneous data aggregation frameworks** to consolidate data from disparate sources (e.g., IoT devices, legacy systems) into a unified digital representation. The integration may involve **ontology-driven alignment** to resolve semantic inconsistencies across data sources.

  This interaction is **passive**—the digital shadow does not autonomously process or act on the data but serves as a **data repository** for downstream analysis or twin-based decision-making.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The technical network infrastructure for Digital Twin systems is designed to support **low-latency, high-reliability communication** between physical and digital components, incorporating the following specifications:
  - **Communication Protocols**: Primarily **OPC UA** (for industrial interoperability) and **MQTT** (for lightweight, publish-subscribe architectures), with support for **TCP/IP** or **UDP** as transport layers. For complex systems, **ROS (Robot Operating System)** or **DDS (Data Distribution Service)** may be employed to enable real-time data exchange.
  - **Network Topology and Architecture**: Typically follows a **hybrid topology** combining **cloud-edge-on-premise** deployments, with **mesh or star architectures** for distributed systems. Critical paths may use **dedicated low-latency networks** (e.g., Ethernet TSN or 5G private networks) to minimize latency.
  - **Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**: Latency constraints are system-dependent (e.g., **<10ms for control loops**, **<1s for monitoring**), with bandwidth demands scaling with data volume (e.g., **100s of Mbps for high-resolution sensor streams**). **Quality of Service (QoS) policies** (e.g., in DDS or MQTT) prioritize critical data flows.
  - **Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms**: Implements **failover protocols** (e.g., **active-active clustering**), **data replication**, and **checksum validation** to ensure continuity. For industrial applications, **ISO 26262-compliant** redundancy may be mandated for safety-critical systems.
  - **Security Protocols**: Enforces **TLS/SSL encryption**, **authentication** (e.g., OAuth 2.0, X.509 certificates), and **access control** (e.g., role-based policies) to protect data integrity. **Zero-trust architectures** and **network segmentation** are recommended for high-security environments.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The deployment infrastructure for Digital Twin systems is optimized for **scalability, performance, and resilience**, leveraging the following technical configurations:
  - **Hosting Platforms**: Supports **multi-cloud (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud)**, **edge computing** (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson, Raspberry Pi clusters), and **on-premise** deployments (e.g., private data centers). **Hybrid cloud-edge architectures** are common for latency-sensitive applications.
  - **Computational and Storage Requirements**: Computational demands vary by use case—**GPU-accelerated workloads** (e.g., for physics simulations in SysML or Simulink) may require **NVIDIA Tesla or AMD Instinct GPUs**, while storage scales from **petabytes for historical data** (e.g., time-series databases like InfluxDB) to **low-latency SSDs for real-time processing**.
  - **Scalability Mechanisms**: Employs **containerization** (e.g., Docker, Kubernetes) for dynamic resource allocation, **auto-scaling policies** (e.g., based on CPU/memory thresholds), and **sharding** for distributed databases. **Serverless architectures** (e.g., AWS Lambda) may be used for sporadic workloads.
  - **Deployment Models Supported**: Includes **monolithic deployments** (for legacy systems), **microservices** (for modular Digital Twin components), and **event-driven architectures** (e.g., using Apache Kafka or AWS EventBridge). **CI/CD pipelines** (e.g., Jenkins, GitLab CI) ensure rapid, automated deployments.
  - **Availability/Reliability Assurance**: Achieved through **multi-region replication**, **disaster recovery plans** (e.g., RTO < 15 mins, RPO < 1 hour), and **high-availability (HA) clusters**. **SLAs** (e.g., 99.99% uptime) are enforced via **monitoring tools** (e.g., Prometheus, Grafana) and **automated failover mechanisms**.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The lifecycle of a Digital Twin (DT) system is structured through **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, encompassing multiple abstraction layers: **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**. The transition between these stages is governed by methodologies such as **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM (Object-Oriented Systems Engineering Methodology)**, **SPES (Systematic Process for Engineering Systems)**, and **SMArDT (Smart Digital Twin Methodology)**. Each layer employs distinct **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like **ACME, AADL, MontiArc, or SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** to model components, interfaces, connectors, and composition. Behavior modeling is further refined using **state machines in SysML v1.7** or **MontiArc Automaton**, while detailed simulations are integrated via tools such as **MathWorks Simulink, general-purpose simulation methods, or code in C++/Java**. Lifecycle governance involves **heterogeneous stakeholder integration** and **multi-tool interoperability**, ensuring seamless transitions between modeling notations and tools across phases. The lifecycle is iterative, with continuous refinement from abstract requirements to executable technical models, adhering to structured **MBSE frameworks** for consistency and traceability.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The **twinning process** and **Digital Twin evolution** are engineered through **MBSE methodologies**, incorporating **quality assurance (QA) practices** embedded within layered modeling (requirements → functional → logical → technical). Development follows **structured methodologies** like **RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT**, which define phased approaches for component decomposition, interface specification, and behavioral modeling. **Version control** is implicitly managed via **tool-specific versioning systems** (e.g., Simulink’s versioning, Git for code artifacts) and **model traceability matrices** to ensure consistency across layers. **Testing and validation** are conducted using **simulation tools (e.g., Simulink, other proprietary simulators)** and **general-purpose languages (C++, Java)** for behavioral validation. **Evolution management** relies on **incremental updates** to models (e.g., refining logical architectures in MontiArc or SysML) and **reconciliation processes** between physical system changes and digital representations. The process emphasizes **iterative refinement** and **stakeholder-driven validation** to align the DT with evolving operational requirements.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin system supports **decision-making** through **analytics-driven insights** generated via **behavioral modeling tools (e.g., SysML state machines, MontiArc Automaton)** and **simulation platforms (e.g., Simulink, other proprietary tools)**. **Insights** are derived from **real-time data aggregation** (as per the Digital Shadow reference model) and **contextual abstraction**, enabling predictive analytics, fault detection, and performance optimization. **User interaction** occurs via **integrated dashboards** (linked to MBSE tools) or **API-based interfaces** for third-party applications. **Decision processes** leverage **model-based reasoning** (e.g., SysML functional analysis) and **simulation-driven scenarios** to evaluate trade-offs. **Confidence measures** for recommendations are implicitly validated through **consistency checks** between physical and digital models, **simulation accuracy metrics**, and **stakeholder-approved validation protocols**. The system does not explicitly mention **machine learning** or **AI-driven insights**, relying instead on **deterministic modeling and simulation**.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The Digital Twin system for the MABI MAX 100 serial robot incorporates multiple **safety-critical and cybersecurity measures** to ensure operational integrity and threat mitigation. The **safety cell** restricts the maximum working range of the first axis to **[-90°, 90°]** and enforces a **maximum speed of |q̇_max| = 75°/s**, preventing unsafe movements. The system employs **dual position encoders** (motor encoder and secondary encoder behind the gearbox) to reduce backlash and positional uncertainty, enabling real-time monitoring of drive train elasticity and friction.

The robot is controlled via the **Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl. CNC controller**, which enforces **fail-safe mechanisms** through strict input/output validation (e.g., position, speed, acceleration) and **authenticated access control** for operational commands. **Torque and current monitoring** (τ_M, I_M) are integrated into the control loop, allowing for **real-time torque feedback** and anomaly detection (e.g., overload or mechanical failure). The system also supports **positional accuracy validation** (±0.1 mm repeatability) and **positional uncertainty reduction** via encoder differential analysis.

For **cybersecurity**, the CNC controller likely implements **hardware-level authentication** (e.g., Siemens’ proprietary security protocols) and **role-based access control** to restrict unauthorized modifications to control parameters. While explicit **risk assessment methodologies** (e.g., ISO 27001, IEC 62443) or **data anonymization techniques** are not detailed, the **closed-loop control architecture** inherently limits exposure of sensitive operational data (e.g., motor current, torque) to external systems unless explicitly configured for telemetry. The **digital shadow** of the first axis (driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor) may require **secure data transmission protocols** (e.g., encrypted communication channels) if integrated into a broader IoT/Digital Twin ecosystem, though this is not explicitly mentioned in the provided context.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
1) _Problem identification:_ To get all relevant requirements,
one has to describe the existing problem, the scope of the
considerations, the scope of the solution, and the purpose
why a DS is created.
2) _Asset and model analysis:_ One has to choose the parts of
the asset that are relevant for the defined DS purpose and
analyze from which data sources the data needed can be
covered. This leads to a description of the asset and the
data structure.

3) _Use or creation of calculations or models:_ As existing
data might not be sufficient to fulfil the purpose, we



can use existing or create new calculations, simulation
models, or data aggregation methods.
4) _Data identification:_ We have to identify relevant data
traces, data points, and their metadata.


III. U SE C ASE


The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX
100 with six axes from the Swiss company MABI Robotic
AG (Fig. 2 top). Due to its flexible rotary joints, high path
repeatability (0 _._ 06 mm) and positional accuracy (0 _._ 1 mm), as
well as its functional reach of 2 m, it is suitable for complex
manufacturing tasks such as machining [44]. The overarching
research task of this industrial robot is machining. The first
vertical robot axis at the base is used for creating a digital
shadow.

Fig. 2 bottom sketches the functional structure. The first
axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor with
the current _I_ _M_ as input and torque _τ_ _M_ as output. To achieve
the necessary low gear ratio, a gear transmission is first used
(with torque _τ_ _G_ as output), followed by a cycloidal gear (with
torque _τ_ _out_ as output). The safety cell of the robot restricts the
maximum working range to [ _−_ 90 _[◦]_ _,_ 90 _[◦]_ ], and the maximum
achievable speed is _|q_ ˙ max _|_ = 75 _[◦]_ _/_ s. There are two position
encoders per drive. The motor encoder is mounted directly
on the motor, the secondary encoder behind the gearbox.
This sensor arrangement can reduce backlash and positional
uncertainty, as the difference between these encoders allows
conclusions to be drawn about the elasticity and friction of the
entire drive train.

The robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D
sl. This Computer Numerical Control (CNC) controller is
particularly suitable for subtractive manufacturing processes
such as milling. The axes can be controlled separately. Position, speed, or acceleration can serve as inputs. Possible
outputs are the target and actual values of the position and its
derivatives, as well as the motor current. The motor current
can be converted into the applied torque using known motor

constants.
